questions.question: A bag contains 4 red balls and 5 blue balls. Two balls are drawn randomly without replacement. Given that the first ball drawn is red, the probability (rounded off to 3 decimal places) that both balls drawn are red is ___________.

understand.plain: We want the conditional probability that the second ball is also red, given the first ball drawn was red. Since drawing is without replacement, after removing one red ball the composition of the bag changes. We use the definition P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B), but the simpler direct method is to note that after one red ball is removed, the updated bag has 3 red and 5 blue balls (8 total).

keywords.explain: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B); probability of A given that B has already occurred.
keywords.example: P(2nd red | 1st red) = P(both red) / P(1st red)

keywords.explain: Once a ball is drawn it is not put back, so the total count decreases and the composition changes.
keywords.example: After drawing 1 red from 4 red + 5 blue, the bag has 3 red + 5 blue = 8 balls remaining.

keywords.explain: Conditioning on the first draw restricts the sample space to the 8 remaining balls.
keywords.example: Given first is red, P(2nd also red) = 3/8 = 0.375

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><rect x='20' y='20' width='120' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Initial Bag</text><text x='80' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>4 red + 5 blue = 9</text><rect x='180' y='20' width='120' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>After 1st Red</text><text x='240' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3 red + 5 blue = 8</text><line x1='140' y1='45' x2='178' y2='45' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><rect x='80' y='95' width='160' height='45' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='160' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P(2nd red | 1st red)</text><text x='160' y='133' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>= 3/8 = 0.375</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes showing bag composition: initial 4 red + 5 blue (9 total), then after drawing first red ball: 3 red + 5 blue (8 total), giving conditional probability 3/8.

given.aim: Find P(2nd ball red | 1st ball red) when drawing without replacement from a bag of 4 red and 5 blue balls.

terms.connects: Determines the updated bag state after the first draw.

terms.meaning: The first ball drawn is not returned, reducing total count to 8.
terms.example: After a red is drawn: 3 red + 5 blue remain.
terms.connects: Changes the denominator and numerator for the second draw.

terms.meaning: Probability of event A given event B has occurred; equals P(A and B)/P(B).
terms.connects: Dividing by P(first red) = 4/9 gives the conditional probability.

given.plan: Use the direct conditioning argument: given first ball is red (3 red balls remain among 8), P(2nd red | 1st red) = 3/8.

steps.title: Identify updated bag after first draw
steps.formula_raw: P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)
steps.apply: First ball is red (given). Remaining bag: 4-1=3 red, 5 blue, total 8 balls.
steps.note: Conditioning on the first draw being red removes one red ball from the count.

steps.title: Compute conditional probability
steps.note: This is valid because all remaining draws are equally likely.

questions.formula_note: Conditional probability under without-replacement sampling is computed by directly reducing the bag count after the given event.

questions.question:
In a certain town, the probability that it will rain in the afternoon is known to be 0.6. Moreover, meteorological data indicates that if the temperature at noon is less than or equal to 25 degrees C, the probability that it will rain in the afternoon is 0.4. The temperature at noon is equally likely to be above 25 degrees C, or below 25 degrees C. What is the probability that it will rain in the afternoon on a day when the temperature at noon is above 25 degrees C?

A. 0.4
B. 0.6
C. 0.8
D. 0.9

understand.plain: We are given the total probability of rain and the conditional probability of rain when temperature is at most 25 degrees. Since temperature is equally split above/below 25 degrees, we can use the Law of Total Probability to find the unknown conditional probability of rain when temperature is above 25 degrees. The two conditional probabilities must average out to give the overall probability.

keywords.explain: P(A) = P(A|B)*P(B) + P(A|B^c)*P(B^c) when B and B^c partition the sample space.
Temperature being above 25 and at most 25 are mutually exclusive and exhaustive events, each with probability 0.5.
Temperature above vs. at most 25 partitions all possible days; their probabilities sum to 1.

understand.visual_alt: No geometric figure needed; this is a total probability calculation.

given.aim: Find P(rain | temp > 25) using the Law of Total Probability.

terms.meaning: Unconditional (total) probability that it rains in the afternoon.
terms.example: This is the marginal probability, averaged over all temperature conditions.
terms.connects: Serves as the known total in the total probability equation.

terms.meaning: Conditional probability of rain given temperature is at most 25 degrees C.
terms.example: On cool days, rain probability is lower: 0.4.
terms.connects: One of the two conditional terms in the total probability expansion.

terms.meaning: Temperature is equally likely above or below 25 degrees C.
terms.example: This symmetric split simplifies the total probability formula.
terms.connects: Weights each conditional probability equally in the total probability formula.

given.plan: Set up P(rain) = P(rain|temp<=25)*0.5 + P(rain|temp>25)*0.5, substitute known values, and solve for the unknown.

steps.title: Write total probability equation
steps.note: The two temperature conditions are complementary and equally probable.

steps.title: Solve for the unknown conditional probability
steps.note: The probability of rain on warm days (0.8) is higher than on cool days (0.4), which is physically intuitive.

questions.formula_note: The Law of Total Probability expresses a marginal probability as a weighted average of conditional probabilities over a partition.

questions.question: A probability density function on the interval [1/e^2, 1] is given by f(x) = a/x, and outside this interval the value of the function is zero. The value of a is ___________.

understand.plain: A valid probability density function must integrate to 1 over its entire support. The function f(x) = a/x is defined on [1/e^2, 1]. We integrate f(x) over this interval and set the result equal to 1 to find the constant a. The integral of 1/x is ln(x), so evaluating between the limits gives the value of a.

keywords.explain: A non-negative function f(x) such that the total area under the curve over the support equals 1.
keywords.example: integral from 1/e^2 to 1 of f(x) dx = 1

keywords.explain: The constant a is chosen so that the total probability equals 1.
integral of 1/x dx = ln(x); ln(1) = 0 and ln(1/e^2) = -2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='270' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='40' y2='20' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='155' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>x</text><text x='25' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>f(x)</text><path d='M60,130 Q100,60 200,40' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><path d='M60,130 Q100,60 200,40 L200,150 L60,150 Z' fill='#CECBF6' opacity='0.5'/><text x='60' y='163' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1/e^2</text><text x='200' y='163' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='135' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f(x)=0.5/x</text><text x='135' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>area = 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph of f(x) = 0.5/x as a decreasing curve over [1/e^2, 1] with the shaded area under the curve equal to 1.

given.aim: Find the constant a such that f(x) = a/x is a valid PDF on [1/e^2, 1].

terms.meaning: The PDF is proportional to 1/x on the given interval, zero elsewhere.
terms.example: This is a scaled log-uniform distribution.
terms.connects: Integrating this over the interval and equating to 1 gives a.

terms.meaning: The support of the PDF; 1/e^2 = e^(-2) approx 0.135.
terms.connects: The width of the log-scale interval determines the normalization constant.

terms.meaning: All probabilities must sum (integrate) to 1 over the support.
terms.connects: This is the single equation needed to uniquely determine a.

given.plan: Integrate a/x from 1/e^2 to 1, set equal to 1, and solve for a.

steps.title: Set up the normalization integral
steps.note: The integral of 1/x is ln(x), a standard calculus result.

steps.title: Evaluate the definite integral
steps.note: ln(1/e^2) = ln(e^{-2}) = -2; the interval spans exactly 2 units on the log scale.

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.note: This confirms f(x) = 0.5/x is a valid PDF on [1/e^2, 1].

questions.formula_note: The normalization condition (total integral equals 1) uniquely determines the scaling constant of any PDF.

questions.question:
Suppose that the expectation of a random variable X is 5. Which of the following statements is true?

A. There is a sample point at which X has the value 5.
B. There is a sample point at which X has value greater than 5.
C. There is a sample point at which X has a value greater than or equal to 5.
D. None of the above.
questions.answer: C. There is a sample point at which X has a value greater than or equal to 5.

understand.plain: The expected value (mean) of a random variable is a weighted average of all its values. A fundamental property is that the expected value cannot exceed the maximum value of the random variable. Equivalently, there must always exist some outcome where the random variable is at least as large as its mean. This is why option C is always true. Options A and B need not hold in general.

keywords.explain: The probability-weighted average of all values X can take; E[X] = sum x_i * P(X = x_i).
keywords.example: If X takes values 3 and 7 each with probability 1/2, E[X] = 5 but X never equals 5.

keywords.explain: The mean is sandwiched between the minimum and maximum of X; no valid distribution can have all values strictly below the mean.
keywords.example: If all values of X were < 5, the weighted average could not equal 5.

keywords.explain: A random variable with E[X]=5 need not take the exact value 5 (ruling out A), and it need not take a value strictly greater than 5 (ruling out B for a degenerate X = 5 always).
keywords.example: X = 5 with probability 1: E[X]=5, no sample point has X > 5 (B is false), but A is true in this edge case only.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the argument is a logical property of the expected value as a weighted average.

given.aim: Determine which statement about a random variable with E[X] = 5 is necessarily true.

terms.meaning: The mean of the distribution of X is 5.
terms.example: X could take 3 with prob 0.5 and 7 with prob 0.5; E[X] = 0.5*3 + 0.5*7 = 5.
terms.connects: The mean is the weighted center of the distribution, bounding the maximum and minimum.

terms.term: Statement A: X = 5 at some sample point
terms.meaning: X must attain exactly the value 5.
terms.example: False in general: X in {3, 7} each with prob 0.5 gives E[X]=5 but X is never exactly 5.

terms.term: Statement C: X >= 5 at some sample point
terms.meaning: At least one outcome must have X at least as large as the mean.
terms.example: If all outcomes had X < 5, the weighted average would be < 5, contradicting E[X]=5.
terms.connects: C is always true by the fundamental property of the expectation.

given.plan: Use the fact that E[X] cannot be strictly greater than all values of X; hence at least one value must be >= E[X] = 5. Check each option and find the one that is always true.

steps.title: Recall the key property of expectation
steps.apply: If every x_i < 5, then E[X] = sum x_i * P(X = x_i) < 5 * sum P(X = x_i) = 5. Contradiction.
steps.note: Therefore at least one sample point must have X >= 5, confirming option C.

steps.title: Rule out options A and B
steps.formula_raw: Counterexample: X in {3, 7}, each with prob 0.5
steps.apply: E[X] = 3*0.5 + 7*0.5 = 5. X never equals 5 (rules out A). For B: if X = 5 always, E[X]=5 but no X > 5 (rules out B).
steps.note: A and B can each fail for specific distributions with E[X]=5.

solution.result: C. There is a sample point at which X has a value greater than or equal to 5.

questions.formula_note: The expected value equals a weighted average of all outcomes, so it must lie within the range [min X, max X]; hence at least one sample point must achieve X >= E[X].

questions.question:
An examination paper has 150 multiple choice questions of one mark each, with each question having four choices. Each incorrect answer fetches -0.25 marks. Suppose 1000 students choose all their answers randomly with uniform probability. The sum total of the expected marks obtained by all these students is

A. 0
B. 2550
C. 7525
D. 9375

understand.plain: Each student answers all 150 questions randomly, choosing uniformly among 4 options. For each question the expected marks is computed using linearity of expectation: the student scores +1 with probability 1/4 (correct) and -0.25 with probability 3/4 (wrong). Multiplying by 150 questions and then by 1000 students gives the total expected marks across all students.

keywords.explain: E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] regardless of dependence; expected total marks = sum of expected marks per question.
keywords.example: E[total per student] = 150 * E[marks per question]
The positive mark (1) is earned with probability 1/4 and the penalty (-0.25) with probability 3/4.

keywords.explain: Each of the 1000 students has the same expected total; total expectation = 1000 * (per-student expectation).

understand.visual_alt: No geometric figure needed; this is an expectation calculation using linearity.

given.aim: Compute the sum of expected marks for 1000 students each randomly answering 150 four-choice MCQs with +1 for correct and -0.25 for incorrect.

terms.meaning: Each question has 4 equally likely choices; random guessing is correct 1 in 4 times.
terms.connects: Together these probabilities determine the expected marks per question.

terms.meaning: Correct answer earns 1 mark; wrong answer loses 0.25 marks.
terms.connects: This expected value per question is positive, so total expected marks > 0.

terms.meaning: Scale factors for the per-question expectation.
terms.connects: Linearity of expectation allows simple multiplication.

given.plan: Compute E[marks per question] = 1*(1/4) + (-1/4)*(3/4). Multiply by 150 questions to get E[per student]. Multiply by 1000 students for the grand total.

steps.title: Expected marks per question for one student
steps.note: The penalty is 1/4 of a mark (-0.25), which equals 3/16 when weighted by the 3/4 wrong-answer probability.

steps.title: Expected marks per student (150 questions)
steps.note: Linearity of expectation allows summing across independent questions.

steps.title: Total expected marks for 1000 students
steps.note: Each student's performance is independent and identically distributed, so expectations add.

questions.formula_note: Linearity of expectation allows computing the total expected marks as a simple product of per-question expectation, number of questions, and number of students.
